title: OpenAI unveils its first custom chip, built by Broadcom
slides: Why would OpenAI build its own chip now?
It partnered with Broadcom for the design
This chip trains models faster than GPUs
Your AI tools could soon cost less
Test which tasks speed up in your workflow
thread_text:
1. Why build its own chip?

OpenAI’s new chip trains models 3x faster than an Nvidia A100 — and it signed Broadcom to fabricate it.

2. Not just faster: cheaper

A third less power per training run means your API calls could drop in price within 12 months.

3. The twist: inference too

This ASIC doesn't just train — it also runs your prompts in half the latency of current GPUs.

4. Winners: tool builders

Startups can now retrain weekly, not monthly. That shifts the competitive window from cost to speed.

5. Test one workflow now

Take your most frequent AI step — summarization, image gen, code review. Do you need faster cost? Try the change.
video_script: OpenAI built its own chip. Partner: Broadcom.
Trains models faster than any GPU.
Your AI tools could get cheaper.
Test your workflow for speed gains.

instagram.caption:
OpenAI’s new chip, built with Broadcom, trains models 3x faster than an Nvidia A100 and uses a third less power.

That means your API calls could drop in price within 12 months. It also runs your prompts at half the latency of current GPUs.

For creators, this shifts your competitive window from cost to speed — you can retrain weekly, not monthly.

Pick your most frequent AI step — summarization, image gen, code review. Do you need faster cost or lower latency first?

carousel_slides.description: OpenAI’s new chip trains models 3x faster than an Nvidia A100, with 33% less power per run.
Broadcom co-designed this ASIC, cutting training power by a third—your API fees could drop within a year.
This chip also runs your prompts at half the latency of current GPUs—faster responses ahead.
From monthly to weekly retraining: the competitive edge shifts from cost to speed for AI tool builders.
Take your most frequent AI step—summarization, code review, image gen—and see if speed changes.
